lifeNote: Your nervous system doesn't know the difference between a real threat and a notification. Treat it accordingly.
headline: Ceasefire fractures as Israel kills 254 in Lebanon; Amazon proves $15B AI revenue at 260x growth
tldr: Israel killed 254 people in Lebanon hours after the ceasefire was announced, oil slipped back below $100 as the Islamabad talks begin today, Amazon revealed a $15 billion AI revenue run rate while committing $200 billion in capex, and Trump told NATO they weren't there when he needed them. The ceasefire is fracturing before the ink dries.

signals.text: Israel launched 'Operation Eternal Darkness' within hours of the ceasefire announcement, killing 254 people across Lebanon in approximately 100 airstrikes using 50 fighter jets and 160 munitions. The dispute over whether Lebanon was included in the ceasefire is now the central fracture line heading into today's Islamabad talks.

Amazon CEO Andy Jassy revealed AWS's AI revenue run rate has hit $15 billion, growing at 260 times the rate AWS experienced at a comparable stage, with customer commitments already covering a 'substantial portion' of the $200 billion in 2026 capex. This converts the AI capex debate from speculation to demonstrated arithmetic.
signals.terminalLine: AWS AI revenue hits $15B at 260x growth

signals.text: The Islamabad talks begin today with VP Vance, Witkoff, and Kushner leading the US side and Iran's Parliament Speaker Ghalibaf and FM Araghchi leading the Iranian side. The ceasefire expires April 22, giving both sides less than two weeks to reach terms, with Lebanon escalation threatening to collapse the entire framework.
signals.terminalLine: Islamabad talks begin today; ceasefire expires Apr 22

signals.text: Morgan Stanley launched MSBT, its spot Bitcoin ETF, at a 0.14% expense ratio that undercuts BlackRock's IBIT (0.25%) and every other US spot BTC fund, placing MSBT in the top 1% of all ETF launches by initial performance. This targets the traditional wealth management client who would never open a Coinbase account.
signals.terminalLine: Morgan Stanley Bitcoin ETF undercuts BlackRock at 0.14%

signals.text: FOMC minutes revealed the Fed is genuinely split, with a 'vast majority' noting inflation progress could be 'slower than previously expected' while some officials emphasized rate decisions as 'two-sided,' explicitly acknowledging both cut and hike possibilities. Oil bouncing back above $100 means the ceasefire hasn't resolved the Fed's bind.
signals.terminalLine: Fed split on rates; inflation slower, but oil complicates

take.title: The $200 Billion Validation
take.subtitle: Why Amazon's Letter Changes the AI Capex Debate from Speculation to Arithmetic
take.preview: For two years, the AI capex buildout has been a bet. Amazon just answered: $15 billion annualized AI revenue at AWS, growing at 260x the rate AWS experienced at a comparable stage, with customer commitments already covering a 'substantial portion' of the $200 billion in 2026 capex. This is not a projection. It's a disclosed run rate. Microsoft reports April 29. Google reports April 30. If either discloses an AI revenue run rate within the same order of magnitude as Amazon's $15 billion, the AI capex debate ends.
take.framework: Revenue Validation Framework: when a dominant platform discloses revenue at scale that converts capex debate from 'is this real?' to 'who else can do this?', the entire upstream and downstream chain reprices.

innerGame.quote: When we are no longer able to change a situation, we are challenged to change ourselves.
innerGame.attribution: Viktor Frankl, Man's Search for Meaning
innerGame.action: Write down, in one sentence, the reason you're doing what you're doing this week. Not the task list. The reason behind it. Then carry that sentence with you through one difficult moment today. Not as motivation. As a compass.

model.name: Giffen Goods
model.preview: A Giffen good is a product where demand RISES as price increases, violating the basic law of supply and demand. The mechanism requires three conditions: (1) the good must be a staple that consumers can't easily substitute, (2) it must consume a large share of the consumer's budget, and (3) the price increase must be large enough that consumers can't afford the higher-quality alternative they'd normally choose. The Giffen mechanism explains why consumer spending data can look 'resilient' while the underlying household economics are deteriorating.
